title: Reading News
intro: As the summer term winds down, here's what's topping your reading logs — Horrible Histories back on stage, a new Michael Morpurgo memoir on the way, this year's Carnegie winners, and the Summer Reading Challenge under way.

items.headline: The Worst Witch — 50 years on and still your most-read series
items.author: Jill Murphy
items.book: A Bad Spell for the Worst Witch
items.summary: Mildred Hubble is comfortably your most-read character across Tally schools. Jill Murphy's series turned 50 in 2024 and the anniversary edition is in shops, with the much-loved CBBC television series to carry on with once the books are finished. A safe recommendation for any child who likes a little magic and mischief.

items.headline: From A Box of Socks to a creepy mansion — Amanda Brandon's new chapter reader
items.author: Amanda Brandon
items.summary: Amanda Brandon — whose A Box of Socks is one of your most-read titles — has The Curse of Raven's Hall out now (Maverick, May 2026): a comic-strip-style illustrated chapter reader set in a spooky old mansion, pitched at ages 5–8. A natural next step for a child who's outgrown her early readers but still wants that warmth and humour.

items.headline: The Nowhere Boy comes from a teacher who knows her readers
items.author: Sandra Glover
items.summary: Sandra Glover was a head of history before she turned to writing, and The Nowhere Boy — one of your most-read titles — was her very first novel, shortlisted for the Lancashire Children's Book of the Year. Its mix of everyday foster-family life and a mysterious boy who speaks straight into Mandy's mind is a gentle way into science fiction for confident readers.

items.headline: Biff, Chip and Kipper — a watch-alike for your earliest readers
items.author: Roderick Hunt
items.summary: The Oxford Reading Tree trio your beginner readers meet have their own screen outings — the CBeebies series Biff & Chip and the classic Magic Key animation — worth pointing families to for a little reading support at home.

items.headline: Michael Morpurgo's most personal book yet — fifty years on a Devon farm
items.author: Michael Morpurgo
items.summary: The author of Running Wild publishes Down Burrow Lane: Life and Love on the Farm on 17 September — a memoir of the fifty years he and his wife Clare spent on the Devon farm that inspired War Horse and Private Peaceful, and where they founded the Farms for City Children charity. One for the staffroom, and lovely background for a class already hooked on his animal stories.

items.headline: Horrible Histories on stage: Awful Egyptians & Terrible Tudors on tour
items.author: Terry Deary
items.summary: The very titles your classes are reading are live on stage: Birmingham Stage Company's Awful Egyptians and Terrible Tudors are touring UK theatres through 2026, with dates running into late October — actors, songs and eye-popping 3D effects. This year marks 20 years of Horrible Histories on stage. A brilliant tie-in for a history topic.

events.name: Summer Reading Challenge — Read to the Beat!
events.blurb: The Reading Agency's free library challenge runs from 4 July in England and Wales (20 June in Scotland), this year pairing stories with songs alongside Universal Music, with illustrations by Harry Woodgate. A great nudge to keep children aged 4–11 reading over the holidays.

events.name: Beatrix Potter's 160th birthday
events.blurb: The creator of Peter Rabbit was born on 28 July 1866 — a lovely prompt for a Potter display or a read-aloud of the little tales with your youngest classes.

events.name: Roald Dahl Day
events.blurb: Marked each year on Dahl's birthday, 13 September. Schools often celebrate with dressing-up, golden tickets and a chapter or two of the classics.

events.name: National Poetry Day — 'Wonder'
events.blurb: Thursday 1 October, this year on the theme of Wonder, with free classroom resources from the Forward Arts Foundation and trained poets going into schools. A perfect prompt for a class poem or a favourite verse read aloud.

events.name: Michael Morpurgo's birthday
events.blurb: One of your most-read authors was born on 5 October — pair it with Running Wild, or his new farm memoir Down Burrow Lane, out the month before.

events.name: World Book Day
events.blurb: The next World Book Day falls on Thursday 4 March 2027. Worth pencilling in early for £1 book tokens, dressing-up and reading-for-pleasure activities.

general.label: Book award
general.text: The 2026 Carnegie Medals were announced in June: Kate Rolfe won the Illustration Medal for Wiggling Words, a picture book about what reading feels like with dyslexia, and Beth O'Brien won the Writing Medal for Wolf Siren.

general.label: Reading research
general.text: Just 1 in 3 children aged 8–18 said they enjoyed reading in their free time in 2025 — a 20-year low — though the National Literacy Trust's 2026 report shows a small rise. Children who enjoy reading report better wellbeing and do better at school, which makes every reading session count.

general.label: Reading research
general.text: OECD analysis found that reading for pleasure is one of the biggest indicators of a child's future success — a stronger predictor than the family's wealth or background.

general.label: Did you know?
general.text: A child who reads for around 20 minutes a day meets roughly 1.8 million words a year — a big part of why regular readers pull ahead.
